words.meaning: Making progress as expected, no delays
words.example: The feature is on track for Friday's release.

words.meaning: Running late compared to the planned timeline
words.example: We are behind schedule due to unexpected API issues.

words.meaning: An important point or goal in a project
words.example: We hit a major milestone by completing the authentication module.

words.meaning: A specific output or result to be completed by a deadline
words.example: The main deliverable for this sprint is the checkout flow.

words.meaning: A rough calculation of time or effort needed
words.example: My estimate for this task is about three days.

speaking.title: 💬 Speaking — Progress Report
speaking.context: Your sprint ends on Friday. Your project manager asks: "Can you give us a quick update on where things stand with the payment integration?" You have finished the backend API (100%), the frontend form (80%), and testing has not started yet. You expect to finish everything by Thursday.
speaking.prompt: Report your progress clearly. Use percentages or status words. Mention what is done, what is in progress, and give a completion estimate.
speaking.sample_answer: Sure! The backend API for payment integration is fully done — 100% complete and already merged. The frontend form is about 80% done, I just need to add form validation. Testing hasn't started yet, but I'm planning to start that tomorrow. Overall, I'm on track to have everything wrapped up by Thursday.

reading.title: 📖 Reading — Effective Progress Updates
reading.passage:
Giving a clear progress update is a critical communication skill for developers. A good update tells stakeholders exactly where a project stands without burying them in technical details.

The best progress reports use concrete numbers and clear status indicators. Instead of saying "I've been working on it," say "I'm 70% done." Instead of "There are some issues," say "I found two bugs that will add one day to the timeline."

Always distinguish between what is complete, what is in progress, and what has not started. This three-part structure helps your audience understand the full picture instantly.

End your update with a clear completion date or a revised estimate. If you are behind schedule, say so early — your team needs time to adjust plans. Surprises are bad; transparency is good.
questions: What should you say instead of 'I've been working on it'?
What three parts should a good progress report include?
answers: Use concrete numbers like 'I'm 70% done' to be specific.
What is complete, what is in progress, and what has not started.

items.phrase: We're on track — should be done by end of week.
items.indonesian: Kita sesuai jadwal — seharusnya selesai akhir minggu ini.

items.phrase: We're slightly behind schedule due to some unexpected issues.
items.indonesian: Kita sedikit terlambat karena ada masalah yang tidak terduga.

items.phrase: The feature is about 60% complete at this point.
items.indonesian: Fitur ini sudah selesai sekitar 60% sampai saat ini.
